content: Let me start by reading the necessary files to understand my task as the lean-auditor subagent.
I'll start by reading the subagent instructions and project context files.

1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.

11	/-!
12	# Weil divisors on a smooth proper curve (RR.1)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.1** sub-build chapter for the project's headline
15	`genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
16	isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).

18	Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `WeilDivisor` on a scheme; adjacent pieces
19	(`MeromorphicOn.divisor`, `CommRing.Pic`, `Scheme.RationalMap`) cover different
20	ground. This file is therefore **project-bespoke**, scaffolding the formal-sum data
21	type `Div(X) = ⨁_{Y ⊂ X codim 1} ℤ` on a Noetherian integral scheme `X` satisfying
22	Hartshorne's condition `(*)`, the principal-divisor homomorphism
23	`div : K(X)^× → Div(X)` on a curve, the degree map `deg : Div(C) → ℤ` on a smooth
24	proper curve over an algebraically closed field, the degree-zero of principal divisors
25	on a complete nonsingular curve (Hartshorne Cor. II.6.10), and the linear-equivalence
26	relation `D ~ D'`.
27	
28	## Status (iter-172 file-skeleton)
29	
30	This file is the **iter-172 Lane C** file-skeleton: each declaration carries the
31	intended signature (matching the blueprint `\lean{...}` pin) with a `sorry` body.
32	The bodies are iter-173+ work after the sibling chapters `RR.2`
33	(`RiemannRoch_RRFormula.tex`), `RR.3` (`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`), and `RR.4`
34	(`RiemannRoch_RationalIsoP1.tex`) land.
35	
36	The 9 pinned declarations are:
37	
38	1. `Scheme.WeilDivisor` — free abelian group on prime divisors (Definition).
39	2. `Scheme.RationalMap.order` — order of a rational function along a prime divisor.
40	3. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint` — Weil divisor associated to a closed point.
41	4. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree` — degree map over an algebraically closed base.
42	5. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.degree_hom` — degree is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
43	6. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal` — principal divisor of a rational function.
44	7. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_hom` — `div` is a group homomorphism (Theorem).
45	8. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.principal_degree_zero` — `deg ∘ div = 0` on a complete curve.
46	9. `Scheme.WeilDivisor.LinearEquivalence` — linear equivalence of Weil divisors.
47	
48	## References
49	
50	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` (445 LOC, 9 pins).
51	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, II §6 (pp. 130–137 + IV.1 pp. 294–296).
52	Stacks Project, tags 02RW (divisors), 02ME (order at a point), 0BE0 (degree),
53	0BE3 (principal divisors have degree zero on a complete nonsingular curve).

56	set_option autoImplicit false

58	universe u
59	
60	open CategoryTheory Limits
61	
62	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
63	
64	/-! ## §1. Codim-1 cycle group / Weil divisor group
65	
66	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` (II §6, p. 130) is: `X` is a Noetherian integral
67	separated scheme that is regular in codimension one. Under `(*)`, prime divisors
68	are closed integral subschemes of codimension one, and the local ring at the
69	generic point of a prime divisor is a DVR with quotient field the function field
70	`K(X)`.
71	
72	A prime divisor is encoded by its **generic point** together with the
73	codimension-one witness `Order.coheight point = 1`: the data field `point : X`
74	selects the generic point of the closed integral subscheme, and the predicate
75	field `coheight` enforces codimension one in the specialisation preorder
76	(Hartshorne II §6 p. 130; blueprint pin `def:prime_divisor`). Integrality of the
77	closure is automatic, so no separate integrality witness is needed.
78	-/
79	
80	/-- A **prime divisor** on a scheme `X`: a closed integral subscheme of codimension
81	one, encoded (for a scheme satisfying Hartshorne's condition `(*)`) by its generic
82	point. On a curve, prime divisors correspond bijectively to closed points.
83	
84	The codimension-one witness is the predicate `Order.coheight point = 1` on the
85	specialisation preorder of `X.carrier` (Mathlib's convention is
86	`x ≤ y ↔ y ⤳ x`, so the generic point is the unique maximal element and a
87	generic point of a codim-1 closed integral subscheme has a length-one chain
88	above it). The closure `{point}̄` is automatically irreducible (so the reduced
89	induced subscheme structure is automatically integral); hence no separate
90	integrality field is needed.
91	
92	Blueprint reference: `def:prime_divisor` / `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6
93	p. 130; Stacks 02RW). -/
94	structure Scheme.PrimeDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) where
95	  /-- The generic point of the closed integral subscheme. -/
96	  point : X
97	  /-- Codimension-1 witness: `point` has coheight `1` in the specialisation
98	  preorder on `X.carrier`. Per iter-173 `wd-spec-refine` (`def:prime_divisor`). -/
99	  coheight : Order.coheight point = 1
100	
101	/-- The **Weil divisor group** of a scheme `X` satisfying Hartshorne's condition
102	`(*)`: the free abelian group on the set of prime divisors of `X`,
103	`Div(X) = ⨁_{Y prime divisor of X} ℤ · Y`. An element `D = Σ nᵢ · Yᵢ` with finitely
104	many nonzero coefficients is a **Weil divisor**; if all `nᵢ ≥ 0` it is **effective**.
105	
106	Blueprint reference: `def:codim1_cycles` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 130). -/
107	def Scheme.WeilDivisor (X : Scheme.{u}) : Type u := X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ
108	
109	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
110	
111	noncomputable instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : AddCommGroup X.WeilDivisor :=
112	  inferInstanceAs (AddCommGroup (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
113	
114	instance (X : Scheme.{u}) : Inhabited X.WeilDivisor :=
115	  inferInstanceAs (Inhabited (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ))
116	
117	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
118	
119	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — PrimeDivisor open-immersion bridge
120	
121	Iter-200 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 1 substrate (per `analogies/wd-stacks02iz.md`
122	and `task_results/mathlib-analogist-wd-stacks02iz.md`): on top of the existing
123	project-side `AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.CoheightBridge`
124	(`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`, axiom-clean iter-183) we package the
125	PrimeDivisor-level open-immersion bijection used by future RR.1 / A.4.a
126	substrate work.
127	
128	The four declarations are:
129	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen` — given a prime divisor `Y` of `X` and
130	  `Y.point ∈ U`, the corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U`.
131	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen` — push a prime divisor of an open subscheme `U`
132	  back to a prime divisor of the ambient scheme `X`.
133	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.equivOpen` — the bijection
134	  `{ Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor`.
135	- `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` — the stalk identification along the
136	  open immersion `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X`, a thin wrapper around Mathlib's
137	  `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`.
138	
139	References: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks), Stacks 005X (coheight ↔ Krull
140	dim on Noetherian schemes); iter-183 substrate at
141	`AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`.
142	-/
143	
144	namespace Scheme.PrimeDivisor
145	
146	variable {X : Scheme.{u}}
147	
148	/-- **Extensionality for `Scheme.PrimeDivisor`.** Two prime divisors with
149	the same underlying point are equal (the coheight witness is a `Prop`-valued
150	field, so it carries no data). Useful for the round-trip lemmas of the
151	`equivOpen` bijection below. -/
152	@[ext]
153	lemma ext {Y Y' : X.PrimeDivisor} (h : Y.point = Y'.point) : Y = Y' := by
154	  cases Y; cases Y'
155	  congr
156	
157	/-- **PrimeDivisor restriction along an open immersion.** Given a prime
158	divisor `Y` of `X` whose generic point lies in an open `U ⊆ X`, the
159	corresponding prime divisor of the open subscheme `U.toScheme`. The
160	codimension-one witness transports via `Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`
161	(iter-183 project substrate, Stacks 02IZ topological side). -/
162	def restrictToOpen (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
163	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
164	  point := ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
165	  coheight := by
166	    rw [← Y.coheight]
167	    exact (Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen Y.point hYU).symm
168	
169	/-- **PrimeDivisor extension from an open subscheme.** A prime divisor of an
170	open subscheme `U.toScheme` lifts to a prime divisor of the ambient `X` along
171	the open immersion `U.ι`. Codimension-one is preserved via the same
172	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding` bridge, this time in the forward
173	direction. -/
174	def ofOpen (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
175	    X.PrimeDivisor where
176	  point := YU.point.1
177	  coheight := by
178	    rw [Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding U.isOpen YU.point.1 YU.point.2]
179	    exact YU.coheight
180	
181	@[simp]
182	lemma restrictToOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
183	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
184	    (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point = ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩ := rfl
185	
186	@[simp]
187	lemma ofOpen_point (U : X.Opens) (YU : U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor) :
188	    (ofOpen U YU).point = YU.point.1 := rfl
189	
190	/-- **Bijection between prime divisors of `X` lying inside `U` and prime
191	divisors of `U.toScheme`.** The forward map is
192	`Y ↦ Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y.val Y.property`; the inverse is
193	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen U`. Both maps are coheight-preserving by
194	`Order.coheight_eq_of_isOpenEmbedding`. -/
195	def equivOpen (U : X.Opens) :
196	    { Y : X.PrimeDivisor // Y.point ∈ U } ≃ U.toScheme.PrimeDivisor where
197	  toFun Y := restrictToOpen U Y.1 Y.2
198	  invFun YU := ⟨ofOpen U YU, YU.point.2⟩
199	  left_inv := by
200	    rintro ⟨Y, hY⟩
201	    rfl
202	  right_inv := by
203	    intro YU
204	    rfl
205	
206	/-- **Stalk identification along an open immersion at a prime divisor.** A
207	thin wrapper around Mathlib's `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso`
208	specialised to the underlying carrier of a prime divisor `Y` lifted from
209	the open subscheme. Stacks 02IZ stalk side. -/
210	noncomputable def stalkIso (U : X.Opens) (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
211	    (hYU : Y.point ∈ U) :
212	    U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk (restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point ≅
213	      X.presheaf.stalk Y.point :=
214	  AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U ⟨Y.point, hYU⟩
215	
216	end Scheme.PrimeDivisor
217	
218	end AlgebraicGeometry
219	
220	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — `Ring.ordFrac` naturality
221	
222	Iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 (HARD BAR + extras). Naturality of
223	`Ring.ord`, `Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom`, and `Ring.ordFrac` (Mathlib's
224	`K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` from `Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`) across a ring
225	isomorphism `e : R ≃+* S` between commutative rings, lifted to a compatible
226	fraction-field isomorphism `e_K : Frac R ≃+* Frac S`.
227	
228	This is the algebraic substrate consumed by iter-202+ Sub-build 3
229	(scheme-level `Scheme.RationalMap.order` naturality across the iter-200
230	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` open-immersion bridge), which in turn closes
231	the non-zero branch of `rationalMap_order_finite_support` (the L535 sorry).
232	
233	References: Stacks 02RV (Hartshorne II.6.1), Stacks 02ME (DVR-of-stalk
234	characterisation at codim-1 points), Stacks 02IZ (stalk under open
235	immersion), Stacks 02MD (Mathlib's `Ring.ordFrac` / `Ring.ord` API).
236	-/
237	
238	namespace Ring
239	
240	/-- **`Ring.ord` naturality under a ring isomorphism.**
241	`ord R x = ord S (e x)`. The length of `R/(x)` as `R`-module equals the
242	length of `S/(e x)` as `S`-module: the `R`-linear equivalence
243	`R/(x) ≃ₗ[R] S/(e x)` (with the `S`-side equipped with `R`-module structure
244	via `e`) transports length across rings using `Module.length_eq_of_surjective`.
245	
246	Project-local because Mathlib `b80f227` ships no `ord` naturality lemma. -/
247	private lemma ord_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
248	    (e : R ≃+* S) (x : R) : Ring.ord R x = Ring.ord S (e x) := by
249	  have heq : Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S) = Ideal.map (e : R →+* S) (Ideal.span {x}) := by
250	    rw [Ideal.map_span, Set.image_singleton]; rfl
251	  letI alg : Algebra R S := e.toRingHom.toAlgebra
252	  haveI : IsScalarTower R S (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
253	    refine ⟨fun r s m => ?_⟩
254	    change (algebraMap R S r * s) • m = (algebraMap R S r) • (s • m)
255	    rw [mul_smul]
256	  let φ_ring : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃+* (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) :=
257	    Ideal.quotientEquiv (Ideal.span {x}) (Ideal.span {e x}) e heq
258	  let φ : (R ⧸ Ideal.span ({x} : Set R)) ≃ₗ[R] (S ⧸ Ideal.span ({e x} : Set S)) := by
259	    refine { φ_ring.toAddEquiv with map_smul' := ?_ }
260	    intro r m
261	    induction m using Quotient.inductionOn with
262	    | h y =>
263	      change Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e (r * y)) = e r • Ideal.Quotient.mk _ (e y)
264	      rw [map_mul]; rfl
265	  unfold Ring.ord
266	  rw [φ.length_eq]
267	  exact Module.length_eq_of_surjective (S := R) (R := S) (M := S ⧸ Ideal.span {e x})
268	    e.surjective
269	
270	/-- **`nonZeroDivisors` is preserved by a ring isomorphism.** Direct from
271	Mathlib's `MulEquivClass.map_nonZeroDivisors`. Project-local convenience
272	wrapper to phrase the result as an `Iff` instead of a `Submonoid.map` equality. -/
273	private lemma nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [CommRing S]
274	    (e : R ≃+* S) (r : R) :
275	    r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R ↔ e r ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := by
276	  rw [← MulEquivClass.map_nonZeroDivisors (e : R ≃* S)]
277	  refine ⟨fun h => ⟨r, h, rfl⟩, fun h => ?_⟩
278	  obtain ⟨r', hr', heq⟩ := h
279	  have : r = r' := e.injective heq.symm
280	  rw [this]; exact hr'
281	
282	/-- **`Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom` naturality under a ring isomorphism.**
283	`ordMonoidWithZeroHom S (e r) = ordMonoidWithZeroHom R r`. Reduces to
284	`ord_ringEquiv` after splitting on `r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R`. -/
285	private lemma ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [Nontrivial R]
286	    [CommRing S] [Nontrivial S] (e : R ≃+* S) (r : R) :
287	    Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom S (e r) = Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom R r := by
288	  unfold Ring.ordMonoidWithZeroHom
289	  simp only [MonoidWithZeroHom.coe_mk, ZeroHom.coe_mk]
290	  by_cases hr : r ∈ nonZeroDivisors R
291	  · have her : e r ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e r).mp hr
292	    simp [hr, her, ord_ringEquiv e r]
293	  · have her : e r ∉ nonZeroDivisors S := fun h =>
294	      hr ((nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e r).mpr h)
295	    simp [hr, her]
296	
297	/-- **`Ring.ordFrac` naturality** — iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 HARD BAR
298	(per PROGRESS.md L108--L122 recipe step (1)).
299	
300	Given a ring iso `e : R ≃+* S` between Noetherian Krull-dim-`≤ 1` integral
301	domains and a compatible fraction-field iso `e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S` (compatibility
302	`e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r)` for `r : R`), the
303	order-of-vanishing function on the fraction fields is invariant:
304	`ordFrac S (e_K x) = ordFrac R x` for any `x : K_R`.
305	
306	Proof: `x = 0` is the junk-zero branch. For `x ≠ 0`, write `x = mk' K_R a b`
307	with `a, b ∈ nonZeroDivisors R` via `IsLocalization.surj`. Then
308	`e_K x = mk' K_S (e a) (e b)` via the compatibility hypothesis, and both
309	sides reduce via Mathlib's `ordFrac_eq_div` to a quotient of
310	`ordMonoidWithZeroHom`-values, which agree by `ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv`. -/
311	private lemma ordFrac_ringEquiv {R S : Type*} [CommRing R] [IsDomain R]
312	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 R]
313	    [CommRing S] [IsDomain S] [IsNoetherianRing S] [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 S]
314	    {K_R K_S : Type*} [Field K_R] [Field K_S]
315	    [Algebra R K_R] [IsFractionRing R K_R]
316	    [Algebra S K_S] [IsFractionRing S K_S]
317	    (e : R ≃+* S) (e_K : K_R ≃+* K_S)
318	    (h_compat : ∀ r : R, e_K (algebraMap R K_R r) = algebraMap S K_S (e r))
319	    (x : K_R) :
320	    Ring.ordFrac S (e_K x) = Ring.ordFrac R x := by
321	  by_cases hx : x = 0
322	  · subst hx; rw [map_zero, map_zero, map_zero]
323	  obtain ⟨⟨a, b⟩, hab⟩ := IsLocalization.surj (nonZeroDivisors R) x
324	  have hb_nzd : (b : R) ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := b.2
325	  have hb_ne : (b : R) ≠ 0 := mem_nonZeroDivisors_iff_ne_zero.mp hb_nzd
326	  have h_inj_R : Function.Injective (algebraMap R K_R) := IsFractionRing.injective R K_R
327	  have hb_K_ne : algebraMap R K_R (b : R) ≠ 0 := by
328	    rw [Ne, ← map_zero (algebraMap R K_R), h_inj_R.eq_iff]
329	    exact hb_ne
330	  have ha_ne : a ≠ 0 := by
331	    intro ha
332	    subst ha
333	    rw [map_zero] at hab
334	    rcases mul_eq_zero.mp hab with hx' | hb_ne'
335	    · exact hx hx'
336	    · exact hb_K_ne hb_ne'
337	  have ha_nzd : a ∈ nonZeroDivisors R := mem_nonZeroDivisors_of_ne_zero ha_ne
338	  have he_b_nzd : e b ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e b).mp hb_nzd
339	  have he_a_nzd : e a ∈ nonZeroDivisors S := (nonZeroDivisors_ringEquiv e a).mp ha_nzd
340	  have hx_mk : x = IsLocalization.mk' K_R a ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩ := by
341	    rw [eq_comm, IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul, hab]
342	  have he_K_x : e_K x = IsLocalization.mk' K_S (e a) ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩ := by
343	    rw [eq_comm]
344	    refine (IsLocalization.mk'_eq_iff_eq_mul).mpr ?_
345	    rw [← h_compat a, ← h_compat b, ← map_mul]
346	    exact congrArg e_K hab.symm
347	  rw [he_K_x, hx_mk]
348	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div S ⟨e a, he_a_nzd⟩ ⟨e b, he_b_nzd⟩]
349	  rw [Ring.ordFrac_eq_div R ⟨a, ha_nzd⟩ ⟨b, hb_nzd⟩]
350	  congr 1
351	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e a
352	  · exact ordMonoidWithZeroHom_ringEquiv e b
353	
354	end Ring

358	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — Function field iso for open immersions
359	
360	Iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 PUSH-BEYOND (step (2) per PROGRESS.md
361	L122--L138): canonical iso `U.toScheme.functionField ≅ X.functionField` for
362	integral `X` and nonempty open `U ⊆ X`, obtained by composing Mathlib's
363	`Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U (genericPoint U.toScheme)` with the transport across
364	the genericPoint equality `(U.ι (genericPoint U.toScheme)) = genericPoint X`
365	delivered by `genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion`.
366	
367	This iso is the bridge that lets `Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv` apply at the
368	scheme level (with `e_K = Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U` and `e =
369	Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU` for a prime divisor `Y` of `X` with
370	`Y.point ∈ U`). The full IsFractionRing-compatibility lemma chaining the
371	two isos is iter-202+ Sub-build 3 scope.
372	
373	Reference: Mathlib `AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField.genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion`
374	+ iter-200 `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso` wrapper. -/
375	
376	/-- **Function field isomorphism along an open immersion** (iter-201 Lane
377	WD-A4a Sub-build 2 PUSH-BEYOND). For an integral scheme `X` and a nonempty
378	open subscheme `U ⊆ X`, the function fields of `U.toScheme` and `X` are
379	canonically isomorphic. -/
380	noncomputable def Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
381	    (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] :
382	    (U.toScheme).functionField ≅ X.functionField :=
383	  (Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U (genericPoint U.toScheme)).trans
384	    (X.presheaf.stalkCongr (by
385	      have heq : ((genericPoint U.toScheme : U.toScheme.carrier).val : X.carrier) =
386	          genericPoint X :=
387	        genericPoint_eq_of_isOpenImmersion U.ι
388	      rw [heq]
389	      exact Inseparable.refl _))

391	/-! ## §2. Order of a rational function at a prime divisor
392	
393	For a scheme `X` satisfying `(*)`, every prime divisor `Y` carries a discrete
394	valuation `v_Y` on the function field `K(X)`. The order of a nonzero rational
395	function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along `Y` is the integer `ord_Y(f) := v_Y(f)`. -/
396	
397	namespace Scheme.RationalMap
398	
399	/-- **Order of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×` along a prime divisor `Y`.**
400	
401	For `X` satisfying Hartshorne's `(*)`, the local ring `O_{X,η}` at the generic
402	point `η` of `Y` is a discrete valuation ring with quotient field the function
403	field `K(X)`, and `ord_Y(f) = v_Y(f) ∈ ℤ` is the value of the associated normalised
404	discrete valuation on `f`.
405	
406	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
407	divisor and `ord_P(f) = v_P(f)` is the standard DVR valuation at `P`.
408	
409	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` (Hartshorne II §6 pp. 130–131; Stacks 02ME).
410	
411	iter-176 body (per analogist `dvr-rationalmap-order`): the body uses Mathlib's
412	`Ring.ordFrac` (the canonical `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom from
413	`Mathlib.RingTheory.OrderOfVanishing.Basic`, Stacks `02MD`) on the stalk
414	`X.presheaf.stalk Y.point`, then projects through `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
415	(the canonical projection with junk-on-zero, `Mathlib.Algebra.GroupWithZero.WithZero`).
416	On `f = 0` this gives `order Y 0 = 0` (junk convention from `WithZero.log_zero`).
417	
418	The required Mathlib typeclasses on the stalk are:
419	- `IsNoetherianRing` — from `[IsLocallyNoetherian X]`.
420	- `IsDomain` ⟹ `Nontrivial` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
421	- `IsFractionRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) X.functionField` — from `[IsIntegral X]`.
422	- `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` — threaded explicitly (the
423	  topological-coheight-to-Krull-dim bridge `Order.coheight Y.point = 1 ⟹
424	  Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` is a Mathlib-upstream-pending
425	  gap; see Stacks `02IZ` / `005X`). -/
426	noncomputable def order {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X]
427	    [IsLocallyNoetherian X] (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
428	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
429	    (f : X.functionField) : ℤ :=
430	  WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f)
431	
432	end Scheme.RationalMap
433	
434	/-! ### Regular-in-codimension-one bridge class
435	
436	Hartshorne's condition `(*)` includes "regular in codimension one", i.e.\ every
437	prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk `O_{X,Y.point}`. Mathlib has no direct
438	typeclass for this; we package it as the project-bespoke class
439	`Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne`, with an instance synthesising the per-`Y`
440	Krull-dim-≤-1 condition required by `Scheme.RationalMap.order`. Blueprint pin
441	("Iter-173+ may introduce a `Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne` predicate to
442	abbreviate this"; chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §2 "Standing hypothesis
443	`(*)` in the Lean encoding"). -/
444	
445	/-- Project-bespoke class encoding Hartshorne's "regular in codimension one"
446	clause of `(*)`: every prime divisor `Y` of `X` has a DVR stalk
447	`O_{X,Y.point}`. The `[IsIntegral X]` precondition makes
448	`IsDomain (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` available so that the
449	`IsDiscreteValuationRing` predicate is well-formed
450	(`AlgebraicGeometry.instIsDomainCarrierStalkCommRingCatPresheafOfIsIntegral`). -/
451	class Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne (X : Scheme.{u}) [IsIntegral X] : Prop where
452	  /-- The defining content: every prime divisor's stalk is a discrete valuation
453	  ring. (This is the precise content of Hartshorne's `(*)`; the weaker
454	  `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` derives via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹
455	  `IsPrincipalIdealRing` ⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain via the bridge
456	  instance below.) -/
457	  out : ∀ Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X, IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)
458	
459	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
460	synthesis can derive `IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for
461	every prime divisor `Y`. -/
462	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instIsDiscreteValuationRingStalk
463	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
464	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
465	    IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
466	  Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
467	
468	/-- **Open-immersion descent for `IsRegularInCodimensionOne`** (iter-200 Lane
469	WD-A4a Sub-build 1 PUSH-BEYOND). The codim-1 regularity hypothesis transports
470	along the open immersion `U.ι : U.toScheme ⟶ X`: prime divisors of `U.toScheme`
471	push to prime divisors of `X` via `Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen`, and Mathlib's
472	`Scheme.Opens.stalkIso` gives the ring iso between stalks. We then transport
473	the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` property via
474	`IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing`.
475	
476	References: Stacks 02IZ (open-immersion stalks), iter-183 project substrate
477	in `AlgebraicJacobian/Albanese/CoheightBridge.lean`. -/
478	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instOpen
479	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
480	    (U : X.Opens) [IsIntegral U.toScheme] :
481	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne U.toScheme := by
482	  refine ⟨fun YU => ?_⟩
483	  haveI hY : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk YU.point.1) :=
484	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ofOpen U YU)
485	  exact IsDiscreteValuationRing.RingEquivClass.isDiscreteValuationRing
486	    (AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.stalkIso U YU.point).symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv
487	
488	/-- Bridge instance: from `[Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]`, typeclass
489	synthesis can derive `Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)` for every
490	prime divisor `Y`, via the `IsDiscreteValuationRing` ⟹ `IsPrincipalIdealRing`
491	⟹ `Ring.KrullDimLE 1` chain (Mathlib's `IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing`
492	+ `IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one`). -/
493	instance Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.instKrullDimLEStalk
494	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
495	    (Y : Scheme.PrimeDivisor X) :
496	    Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
497	  haveI : IsDiscreteValuationRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
498	    Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne.out Y
499	  haveI : IsPrincipalIdealRing (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) :=
500	    IsDiscreteValuationRing.toIsPrincipalIdealRing
501	  IsPrincipalIdealRing.krullDimLE_one _
502	
503	/-- **Scheme-side packaging of `Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv` for the open-immersion
504	PrimeDivisor stalk iso** (iter-201 Lane WD-A4a Sub-build 2 PUSH-BEYOND
505	packaging).
506	
507	This is the iter-202+ Sub-build 3 entry point: it pipes the iter-200
508	`Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU` ring iso plus a user-supplied
509	function-field iso `e_K` (typically the `Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U`)
510	and a compatibility hypothesis `h_compat` into the algebraic naturality
511	`Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv`.
512	
513	The full Sub-build 3 closure replaces `e_K` with `Scheme.Opens.functionFieldIso U`
514	and discharges `h_compat` from the naturality of `Scheme.Opens.stalkIso` w.r.t.
515	`stalkSpecializes` to the generic point. That naturality lemma is iter-202
516	scope (it requires either a `Scheme.Hom.stalkSpecializes_stalkMap` analogue or a
517	direct germ-universal chase via Mathlib's
518	`AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Opens.germ_stalkIso_hom_assoc`). -/
519	private theorem Scheme.PrimeDivisor.ordFrac_stalkIso_naturality
520	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
521	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X]
522	    (U : X.Opens) [Nonempty U] [IsIntegral U.toScheme]
523	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne U.toScheme]
524	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) (hYU : Y.point ∈ U)
525	    (e_K : U.toScheme.functionField ≃+* X.functionField)
526	    (h_compat : ∀ r : U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk
527	        (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point,
528	      e_K (algebraMap _ U.toScheme.functionField r) =
529	        algebraMap _ X.functionField
530	          ((Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv r))
531	    (f : U.toScheme.functionField) :
532	    Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (e_K f) =
533	      Ring.ordFrac (U.toScheme.presheaf.stalk
534	        (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.restrictToOpen U Y hYU).point) f :=
535	  Ring.ordFrac_ringEquiv
536	    (Scheme.PrimeDivisor.stalkIso U Y hYU).commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv
537	    e_K h_compat f

539	/-! ### Order-on-curve algebraic identities
540	
541	iter-198 §2 substrate lemmas (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). These are
542	axiom-clean per-prime-divisor algebraic identities on
543	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` — direct consequences of `Ring.ordFrac` being
544	a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero hom composed with `WithZero.log : ℤᵐ⁰ → ℤ`
545	(which is additive on nonzero arguments, junk-zero on `0`).
546	
547	The lemmas:
548	
549	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero`: `ord_Y 0 = 0` (junk convention).
550	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero`: `ord_Y (f·g) = ord_Y f +
551	  ord_Y g` when both `f, g ≠ 0`.
552	- `Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv`: `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a
553	  unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
554	
555	Blueprint reference: `def:order_at_point` §2 (Hartshorne II §6 valuation
556	identities `v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f)+v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(f⁻¹) = -v_Y(f)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`). -/
557	
558	/-- `ord_Y 0 = 0` by the junk-on-zero convention.
559	
560	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_zero`)
561	and `WithZero.log_zero`. -/
562	@[simp]
563	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_zero
564	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
565	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
566	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
567	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (0 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
568	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
569	  rw [map_zero, WithZero.log_zero]
570	
571	/-- `ord_Y (f · g) = ord_Y f + ord_Y g` when `f, g ≠ 0`.
572	
573	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_mul`)
574	and `WithZero.log_mul` (which requires both factors nonzero). -/
575	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero
576	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
577	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
578	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
579	    {f g : X.functionField} (hf : f ≠ 0) (hg : g ≠ 0) :
580	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f * g) =
581	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y g := by
582	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
583	  rw [map_mul]
584	  exact WithZero.log_mul ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hf) ((map_ne_zero _).mpr hg)
585	
586	/-- `ord_Y f⁻¹ = -ord_Y f` for any `f : K(X)`.
587	
588	The `f = 0` case is junk-vacuous (`f⁻¹ = 0` in a `GroupWithZero`, both sides
589	are zero). For `f ≠ 0` this is the DVR-valuation identity `v(f⁻¹) = -v(f)`.
590	
591	Direct from `Ring.ordFrac _` being a monoid-with-zero hom (`map_inv₀`)
592	and `WithZero.log_inv` (which holds for all `WithZero (Multiplicative G)`
593	elements including zero, by the junk-on-zero convention). -/
594	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv
595	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
596	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
597	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
598	    (f : X.functionField) :
599	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f⁻¹ = -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
600	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
601	  rw [map_inv₀, WithZero.log_inv]
602	
603	/-- `ord_Y u⁻¹ = -ord_Y u` for a unit `u : K(X)ˣ`.
604	
605	A specialisation of `order_inv` to the unit form
606	`((u⁻¹ : K(X)ˣ) : K(X)) = (u : K(X))⁻¹`, useful at call sites that thread
607	`Units` (e.g. the `principal_hom : K(X)ˣ →* Multiplicative Div(X)` body). -/
608	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_units_inv
609	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
610	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
611	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
612	    (u : (X.functionField)ˣ) :
613	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
614	      -Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (u : X.functionField) := by
615	  rw [show ((u⁻¹ : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField) =
616	        (u : X.functionField)⁻¹ from by simp]
617	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_inv Y _
618	
619	/-- **`ord_Y (-f) = ord_Y f`** for any rational function `f`.
620	
621	The argument: `(-f)^2 = f^2`, so taking `ordFrac` (a monoid-with-zero hom) and
622	then `WithZero.log_pow` gives `2 • ord_Y (-f) = 2 • ord_Y f` in `ℤ`. The free
623	action `(· • ·) : ℕ → ℤ → ℤ` cancels `2 ≠ 0`, giving the equality.
624	
625	iter-199 §2 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). Foundational
626	sign-flip identity for downstream consumers (Hartshorne II.6.10 non-constant
627	branch, ramification-inertia chase). -/
628	@[simp]
629	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_neg
630	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
631	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
632	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)]
633	    (f : X.functionField) :

637	  have h1 : (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (-f)) ^ 2 =
638	      (Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2 := by
639	    rw [← map_pow, ← map_pow, h]
640	  have h2 : ((Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) (-f)) ^ 2).log =
641	      ((Ring.ordFrac (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point) f) ^ 2).log := by rw [h1]
642	  rw [WithZero.log_pow, WithZero.log_pow] at h2
643	  exact (smul_right_injective ℤ (by norm_num : (2 : ℕ) ≠ 0)) h2

645	/-- **`ord_Y (f^n) = n · ord_Y f`** for a nonzero rational function `f` and
646	natural number exponent `n`.

656	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_pow_of_ne_zero
657	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
658	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)

661	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (f ^ n) = n * Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
662	  induction n with
663	  | zero => simp [Scheme.RationalMap.order]
664	  | succ k ih =>
665	    rw [pow_succ, Scheme.RationalMap.order_mul_of_ne_zero Y (pow_ne_zero k hf) hf, ih]

667	    ring

750	/-! ## §3. Divisor of a closed point on a curve
751	
752	On a smooth proper curve `C` over a field, every closed point `P ∈ C` is a prime
753	divisor (it is closed, integral, of codimension one in the one-dimensional integral
754	scheme `C`). The associated Weil divisor is `[P] = 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`. -/
755	
756	/-- **The Weil divisor associated to a closed point `P` on a curve.** The element
757	`[P] := 1 · P ∈ Div(C)`, i.e. the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one.
758	
759	For a smooth proper curve every closed point is automatically a prime divisor (it
760	is a codimension-one integral closed subscheme of the one-dimensional integral
761	scheme `C`); conversely every prime divisor on a curve is of this form, so an
762	arbitrary divisor on `C` is a finite formal `ℤ`-linear combination
763	`Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` of closed points.
764	
765	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_closed_point` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137).
766	
767	iter-174 body: the function is junk-defined outside its intended scope. We
768	case-split on `Order.coheight P = 1` (the codimension-one witness of
769	`PrimeDivisor`). On the branch where the equality holds — automatic for a
770	closed point on a one-dimensional integral scheme — we promote `P` to a
771	`PrimeDivisor` via the witness and return `Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1`, i.e.
772	the prime divisor `P` with multiplicity one. On the off-branch (junk regime)
773	we return the zero divisor. The blueprint pins the well-definedness argument
774	"`IsClosed {P}` on a one-dimensional integral scheme ⟹ `coheight P = 1`" as a
775	separate threadable hypothesis at the call site (chapter L330–L340 "Lean
776	signature scope"); see `ofClosedPoint_eq_single` for the equation in the
777	hypothesised regime. -/
778	noncomputable def ofClosedPoint {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
779	    (_hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) : C.WeilDivisor :=
780	  if h : Order.coheight P = 1 then Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 else 0
781	
782	/-- In the hypothesised regime where the closed point `P` has coheight one
783	(the codim-1 condition automatic for a closed point of a one-dimensional
784	integral scheme), `ofClosedPoint P hP` is the prime divisor `P` with
785	multiplicity one. -/
786	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_single {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
787	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P = 1) :
788	    ofClosedPoint P hP = Finsupp.single ⟨P, h⟩ 1 := by
789	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]
790	
791	/-- Off-branch: outside the codim-1 regime, `ofClosedPoint` is junk-defined as
792	the zero divisor. (Only relevant when the user supplies a "closed point" that
793	does not have coheight one in the ambient scheme — e.g. a generic point or a
794	codim-≥2 point on a higher-dimensional scheme.) -/
795	lemma ofClosedPoint_eq_zero {C : Scheme.{u}} (P : C)
796	    (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C)) (h : Order.coheight P ≠ 1) :
797	    ofClosedPoint P hP = 0 := by
798	  simp [ofClosedPoint, h]

800	/-! ## §4. Degree of a divisor over an algebraically closed base -/
801	
802	/-- **Degree of a Weil divisor on a smooth proper curve over `k̄`.**
803	
804	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every closed point of a smooth proper
805	curve `C` has residue field `k̄`, so each prime divisor `[P]` contributes degree
806	one to the sum, and `deg(D) := Σᵢ nᵢ` is the sum of the integer coefficients of
807	the formal sum `D = Σ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]`.
808	
809	(Over a general field `k` one weights by the residue-field degrees
810	`Σᵢ nᵢ · [κ(Pᵢ) : k]` to recover the geometric degree; the project's RR bridge
811	needs only the `k̄` specialisation.)
812	
813	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 137; Stacks 0BE0).
814	
815	Implementation: `Finsupp.sum D (fun _ n => n)` is the sum of all coefficients of
816	the finitely supported function representing `D`. -/
817	noncomputable def degree (D : X.WeilDivisor) : ℤ :=
818	  (D : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ).sum (fun _ n => n)
819	
820	/-- **The degree map is a group homomorphism `Div(C) → ℤ`.**
821	
822	`deg(D₁ + D₂) = deg(D₁) + deg(D₂)`, `deg(-D) = -deg(D)`, `deg(0) = 0`. Bundled
823	as an `AddMonoidHom` for downstream use (the linear-equivalence quotient
824	`Cl(C) := Div(C) / im(div)` will inherit a `deg : Cl(C) → ℤ` from this).
825	
826	Blueprint reference: `thm:divisor_degree_hom` (immediate from `def:divisor_degree`).
827	
828	Implementation: built from `Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)`, the
829	generic Mathlib packaging that lifts a family of `AddMonoidHom`s indexed by the
830	support into a single `AddMonoidHom` on the finsupp. Unfolds to
831	`D.sum (fun _ z ↦ z) = degree D` (see `degree_hom_apply` below). -/
832	noncomputable def degree_hom : X.WeilDivisor →+ ℤ :=
833	  Finsupp.liftAddHom (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ)
834	
835	@[simp]
836	lemma degree_hom_apply (D : X.WeilDivisor) : degree_hom D = degree D :=
837	  Finsupp.liftAddHom_apply (α := X.PrimeDivisor) (M := ℤ) (N := ℤ)
838	    (fun _ ↦ AddMonoidHom.id ℤ) D
839	
840	/-- **Degree of the zero divisor is zero.** A direct consequence of
841	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`. -/
842	@[simp]
843	lemma degree_zero : degree (0 : X.WeilDivisor) = 0 := by
844	  rw [← degree_hom_apply]; exact map_zero _
845	
846	/-- **Degree is additive.** A direct consequence of `degree_hom` being an
847	`AddMonoidHom`. -/
848	lemma degree_add (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
849	    degree (D₁ + D₂) = degree D₁ + degree D₂ := by
850	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
851	  exact map_add _ _ _
852	
853	/-- **Degree of the negation.** `deg(-D) = -deg D`. A direct consequence of
854	`degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
855	
856	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
857	@[simp]
858	lemma degree_neg (D : X.WeilDivisor) :
859	    degree (-D) = -degree D := by
860	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
861	  exact map_neg _ _
862	
863	/-- **Degree is subtractive.** `deg(D₁ - D₂) = deg D₁ - deg D₂`. A direct
864	consequence of `degree_hom` being an `AddMonoidHom`.
865	
866	iter-198 §4 substrate sharpening (Lane WD-A4a PUSH-BEYOND). -/
867	lemma degree_sub (D₁ D₂ : X.WeilDivisor) :
868	    degree (D₁ - D₂) = degree D₁ - degree D₂ := by
869	  rw [← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply, ← degree_hom_apply]
870	  exact map_sub _ _ _

872	/-! ## §5. Principal divisors -/
873	
874	/-- **The principal divisor of a nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(X)^×`.**
875	
876	By Hartshorne's Lemma 6.1, `ord_Y(f) = 0` for all but finitely many prime
877	divisors `Y`, so the formal sum
878	`div(f) := Σ_{Y prime divisor} ord_Y(f) · Y ∈ Div(X)`
879	has finite support and is a well-defined Weil divisor.
880	
881	On a smooth proper curve `C` over `k̄`, this specialises to
882	`div(f) = Σ_{P closed point} ord_P(f) · [P]`.
883	
884	Blueprint reference: `def:principal_divisor` (Hartshorne II §6 Lemma 6.1 +
885	following definition, p. 131).
886	
887	iter-177 body: the construction uses `Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` with the
888	finite-support witness `rationalMap_order_finite_support`. The latter is a
889	private theorem packaging Hartshorne 6.1; its body is a Mathlib-pending gap
890	(see chapter `RiemannRoch_WeilDivisor.tex` §5 sub-build note) and is left as
891	a `sorry` for an iter-178+ Mathlib-upstream PR. -/
892	noncomputable def principal [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
893	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField)
894	    (_hf : f ≠ 0) : X.WeilDivisor :=
895	  Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
896	    (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
897	    (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)
898	
899	/-- **The coefficient of `principal f hf` at a prime divisor `Y` is the order of
900	`f` along `Y`.** This is the basic structural unfolding of the
901	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite` packaging in `principal`; one-line via
902	`Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe` from `Mathlib.Data.Finsupp.Defs`.
903	
904	iter-193 substrate helper for the Lane I body close
905	(`degree_positivePart_principal_eq_finrank`). -/
906	lemma principal_apply [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
907	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] (f : X.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0)
908	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor) :
909	    (show (X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) from principal f hf) Y =
910	      Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f := by
911	  change (Finsupp.ofSupportFinite
912	      (fun Y : X.PrimeDivisor => Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f)
913	      (rationalMap_order_finite_support f)) Y = _
914	  rw [Finsupp.ofSupportFinite_coe]
915	
916	/-- **`Scheme.RationalMap.order Y 1 = 0`** — the order of the constant
917	function `1` is `0` at every prime divisor. Direct from
918	`map_one` of `Ring.ordFrac` + `WithZero.log_one`.
919	
920	iter-193 substrate helper for further structural results
921	(`principal_one_eq_zero` and similar). -/
922	@[simp]
923	lemma _root_.AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RationalMap.order_one
924	    {X : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
925	    (Y : X.PrimeDivisor)
926	    [Ring.KrullDimLE 1 (X.presheaf.stalk Y.point)] :
927	    Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (1 : X.functionField) = 0 := by
928	  unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
929	  rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
930	
931	/-- **The principal divisor of the constant function `1` is the zero
932	divisor.** A direct consequence of `Scheme.RationalMap.order_one`. -/
933	@[simp]
934	lemma principal_one [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
935	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
936	    principal (1 : X.functionField) one_ne_zero = 0 := by
937	  change (_ : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) = (0 : X.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)
938	  apply Finsupp.ext
939	  intro Y
940	  rw [principal_apply]
941	  exact Scheme.RationalMap.order_one Y

943	/-- **The principal-divisor map is a group homomorphism `K(X)^× → Div(X)`.**
944	
945	Concretely `div(fg) = div(f) + div(g)`, `div(f⁻¹) = -div(f)`, `div(1) = 0`. Bundled
946	as a `MonoidHom` from the multiplicative units of `K(X)` to `Multiplicative (Div(X))`
947	(equivalently: an additive map `(K(X)^×, ·) → (Div(X), +)`).
948	
949	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_hom` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 131).
950	
951	iter-177 body: closes coordinate-wise from the DVR identities
952	`v_Y(fg) = v_Y(f) + v_Y(g)`, `v_Y(1) = 0`. The per-`Y` identities live in
953	`Scheme.RationalMap.order` via `Ring.ordFrac` (a `K →*₀ ℤᵐ⁰` monoid-with-zero
954	hom) and `WithZero.log_mul` / `WithZero.log_one`. -/
955	noncomputable def principal_hom [IsIntegral X] [IsLocallyNoetherian X]
956	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne X] :
957	    (X.functionField)ˣ →* Multiplicative X.WeilDivisor where
958	  toFun u :=
959	    Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal (↑u : X.functionField) (Units.ne_zero u))
960	  map_one' := by
961	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑1 _) = 1.
962	    -- Use `← ofAdd_zero` to rewrite RHS as `Multiplicative.ofAdd 0`,
963	    -- then `congr 1` reduces to `principal ↑1 _ = 0` (Finsupp equality),
964	    -- and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate identity, which
965	    -- closes by unfolding `order` to `WithZero.log (Ring.ordFrac _ 1) = 0`
966	    -- via `map_one` and `WithZero.log_one`.
967	    rw [← ofAdd_zero]
968	    congr 1
969	    apply Finsupp.ext
970	    intro Y
971	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y
972	        (((1 : (X.functionField)ˣ) : X.functionField)) = 0
973	    rw [Units.val_one]
974	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
975	    rw [map_one, WithZero.log_one]
976	  map_mul' u v := by
977	    -- Goal: Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑(u*v) _) =
978	    --       Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑u _) * Multiplicative.ofAdd (principal ↑v _).
979	    -- Use `← ofAdd_add` to rewrite RHS, then `congr 1` reduces to
980	    -- `principal ↑(u*v) _ = principal ↑u _ + principal ↑v _` (Finsupp
981	    -- equality), and `Finsupp.ext` peels to the per-`Y` coordinate
982	    -- identity `order Y (uv) = order Y u + order Y v`, which closes by
983	    -- `Ring.ordFrac (uv) = Ring.ordFrac u * Ring.ordFrac v` (`map_mul`)
984	    -- and `WithZero.log_mul` (the nonzero hypotheses come from
985	    -- `Units.ne_zero` and `map_ne_zero`).
986	    rw [← ofAdd_add]
987	    congr 1
988	    apply Finsupp.ext
989	    intro Y
990	    change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y ((↑(u * v) : X.functionField))
991	      = Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑u : X.functionField)
992	      + Scheme.RationalMap.order Y (↑v : X.functionField)
993	    rw [Units.val_mul]
994	    unfold Scheme.RationalMap.order
995	    rw [map_mul]
996	    exact WithZero.log_mul
997	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero u))
998	      ((map_ne_zero _).mpr (Units.ne_zero v))

1000	/-- **Principal divisors on a complete nonsingular curve have degree zero**
1001	(Hartshorne Corollary II.6.10, Stacks 0BE3).
1002	
1003	For every nonzero rational function `f ∈ K(C)^×` on a smooth proper curve `C`
1004	over an algebraically closed field `k̄`,
1005	`deg(div(f)) = 0 ∈ ℤ`.
1006	
1007	Blueprint reference: `thm:principal_deg_zero` (Hartshorne II.6 Cor. 6.10 p. 138).
1008	
1009	The proof (Hartshorne 6.10): if `f ∈ k̄` is constant then `div(f) = 0` and the
1010	claim is trivial. Otherwise the inclusion `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` exhibits `K(C)` as a
1011	finite extension of `k̄(f) ≅ k̄(t)`, so the corresponding morphism
1012	`φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` is finite, `div(f) = φ^*([0] - [∞])`, and pullback along a
1013	finite morphism multiplies degree by `deg(φ)`. Two auxiliary sub-lemmas
1014	(finite morphism induced by a non-constant rational function; multiplicativity
1015	of degree under finite pullback, Hartshorne II.6.9) are deferred to follow-up
1016	iters of `RR.1`. -/
1017	theorem principal_degree_zero {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
1018	    (C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))) [IsProper C.hom]
1019	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
1020	    [IsIntegral C.left] [IsLocallyNoetherian C.left]
1021	    [Scheme.IsRegularInCodimensionOne C.left]
1022	    (f : C.left.functionField) (hf : f ≠ 0) :
1023	    degree (principal f hf) = 0 := by
1024	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10 (iter-178 partial). Case-split on whether every
1025	  -- prime-divisor order of `f` vanishes. On a complete nonsingular curve,
1026	  -- this case split exactly recovers Hartshorne's constant-vs-non-constant
1027	  -- split: `(∀ Y, ord_Y f = 0)` ⟺ `f ∈ k̄ \ {0}` (a nowhere-vanishing
1028	  -- rational function on a complete curve is constant; that direction is
1029	  -- Hartshorne II.6.10's "if `f ∈ k̄` then `(f) = 0`").
1030	  by_cases hconst : ∀ Y : C.left.PrimeDivisor, Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
1031	  · -- Constant branch: every order vanishes ⟹ `principal f hf = 0` ⟹
1032	    -- `degree (principal f hf) = degree 0 = 0`.
1033	    have hprincipal_zero : principal f hf = 0 := by
1034	      apply Finsupp.ext
1035	      intro Y
1036	      change Scheme.RationalMap.order Y f = 0
1037	      exact hconst Y
1038	    rw [hprincipal_zero]
1039	    unfold degree
1040	    exact Finsupp.sum_zero_index
1041	  · -- Non-constant branch (Hartshorne II.6.10 `f ∉ k̄`): the inclusion
1042	    -- `k̄(f) ⊂ K(C)` defines a finite morphism `φ : C → ℙ¹_{k̄}` via the
1043	    -- function-field-determines-curve correspondence (Hartshorne I.6.12),
1044	    -- and `principal f hf = φ^*([0] - [∞])`. Pullback along a finite
1045	    -- morphism of curves multiplies degrees by `deg(φ)` (Hartshorne
1046	    -- II.6.9), so `deg(principal f hf) = deg(φ) · deg([0] - [∞]) =
1047	    -- deg(φ) · 0 = 0`.
1048	    -- This branch is gated on (i) the `φ : C → ℙ¹` construction (Lane 5
1049	    -- `RationalCurveIso.lean:morphismToP1OfGlobalSections`, iter-178+),
1050	    -- and (ii) the degree-multiplicativity-under-finite-pullback bridge
1051	    -- (Hartshorne II.6.9, Mathlib gap).

9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27

29	
30	This file was originally landed iter-175 (Lane F file-skeleton) with each

32	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in

42	`CohenMacaulay.of_regular`) are substantive multi-iter content and stay

46	
47	1. `RingTheory.Module.depth` (noncomputable def, ~6 LOC) — the `I`-depth of a
48	   finite `R`-module as the supremum of lengths of `M`-regular sequences in

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory

157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory
193	
194	namespace Module
